stem: The single most effective measure for preventing the spread of infection in a long-term care facility is:
choice_a: Wearing gloves during all resident contact
choice_b: Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times
choice_c: Using a surgical mask during all care
choice_d: Isolating all residents with known infections
rationale: Hand hygiene is universally recognized as the single most effective infection prevention measure.
source_reference: General CNA infection control curriculum - NEEDS SME REVIEW (Module 1)

stem: Which of the following is the correct order for removing (doffing) PPE after caring for a resident on contact precautions?
choice_a: Mask then gloves then gown then eye protection
choice_b: Gloves then eye protection/face shield then gown then mask
choice_c: Gown then gloves then mask then eye protection
choice_d: Eye protection then mask then gown then gloves
rationale: CDC/WHO-recommended doffing removes most contaminated items first (gloves) then eye protection then gown then mask.
source_reference: General CNA infection control curriculum - NEEDS SME REVIEW (Module 1)

stem: Standard Precautions require you to treat which of the following as potentially infectious?
choice_a: Only blood and saliva
choice_b: Only body fluids from residents with known infections
choice_c: All blood and body fluids and non-intact skin and mucous membranes from all residents
choice_d: Body fluids only when a resident is visibly ill
rationale: Standard Precautions apply to all residents at all times regardless of diagnosis.
source_reference: General CNA infection control curriculum - NEEDS SME REVIEW (Module 1)

stem: A fictional resident Mr. Carter tells you that a staff member has been taking money from his wallet. This is an example of:
choice_a: Neglect
choice_b: Verbal abuse
choice_c: Financial abuse
choice_d: Psychological abuse
rationale: Taking money from a resident without consent is financial abuse.

stem: As a California CNA you are a mandated reporter. This means you must report suspected abuse:
choice_a: Only after you have collected evidence to prove it occurred
choice_b: Only if you personally witnessed it
choice_c: Immediately based on reasonable suspicion - certainty is not required
choice_d: Only after your supervisor gives you permission
rationale: Mandated reporters must report on reasonable suspicion. Certainty and personal witness are not prerequisites.

stem: Leaving a resident unattended or failing to plan for their care is called:
choice_a: Neglect
choice_b: Abandonment
choice_c: Involuntary seclusion
choice_d: Negligence
rationale: Abandonment is defined as leaving a resident unattended or failing to plan for their care.

stem: Which of the following BEST describes the correct action when you observe a possible abuse situation?
choice_a: Investigate the situation yourself before reporting to avoid false accusations
choice_b: Ensure resident safety then report to the licensed nurse immediately and document objective observations
choice_c: Wait to see if the behavior happens again before reporting
choice_d: Report it to a coworker and ask them to handle it
rationale: The CNA ensures safety then reports immediately and documents objectively. Investigating and waiting are inappropriate.

stem: Every resident in a long-term care facility has the right to:
choice_a: Have their care decisions made only by their physician
choice_b: Be free from physical or chemical restraints used for staff convenience
choice_c: Have their personal mail opened and read by staff for safety
choice_d: Share a room with any resident the facility assigns without discussion
rationale: Freedom from restraints used for staff convenience is a fundamental resident right.
source_reference: NATP Module 17 (Patient/Resident Abuse); NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents)

stem: A resident with Alzheimer's disease repeatedly asks you what day it is even after you have answered. The BEST response is to:
choice_a: Tell the resident to write it down so they remember
choice_b: Remind the resident that you already told them
choice_c: Answer patiently each time - the resident genuinely cannot retain the information
choice_d: Ignore the question after the second time to discourage repetition
rationale: Repetitive questioning in dementia is caused by memory loss. Patient repetition is appropriate.
source_reference: NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents) Objective 5

stem: Which of the following is an example of validating a resident's emotion during a dementia care interaction?
choice_a: Your husband passed away ten years ago. He is not coming home.
choice_b: You miss your husband. Tell me about him - what was he like?
choice_c: You need to stop worrying. Everything is fine here.
choice_d: I already told you - your husband is not coming.
rationale: Validation acknowledges the emotion and redirects through reminiscence without arguing about facts.
source_reference: NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents) Objective 5

stem: The five stages of grief identified by Elisabeth Kubler-Ross are:
choice_a: Shock then sadness then anger then fear then recovery
choice_b: Denial then anger then bargaining then depression then acceptance
choice_c: Confusion then denial then guilt then mourning then closure
choice_d: Fear then anger then surrender then peace then hope
rationale: The Kubler-Ross stages are denial anger bargaining depression and acceptance.

stem: Which of the following best describes hospice care?
choice_a: Aggressive treatment aimed at curing the terminal illness
choice_b: Comfort-focused care for individuals with a life expectancy of six months or less
choice_c: Care provided only in a hospital setting
choice_d: A program for residents who have refused all medical treatment
rationale: Hospice is comfort-focused care for those with a life expectancy of six months or less.

stem: Sudden confusion in a resident who has been mentally stable is most likely a sign of:
choice_a: Normal progression of dementia
choice_b: Delirium possibly caused by infection or medication change or dehydration
choice_c: A personality disorder
choice_d: The resident being intentionally difficult
rationale: Sudden-onset confusion in a stable resident is a hallmark of delirium which is often reversible.
source_reference: NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents) Objective 5

stem: When assisting a resident to ambulate the CNA should stand:
choice_a: Directly in front of the resident
choice_b: Slightly behind and to the side of the resident's stronger side
choice_c: Slightly behind and to the side of the resident's weaker side
choice_d: Several feet away to encourage independence
rationale: The CNA stands on the weaker side to support and prevent falls if balance is lost toward that side.

stem: Before performing passive range of motion (PROM) exercises the CNA should:
choice_a: Force the joint through the full range to maximize effectiveness
choice_b: Only exercise the resident's stronger limbs
choice_c: Check the care plan and support the limb above and below the joint being exercised
choice_d: Ask the resident to perform the movements independently first
rationale: Check the care plan. Support above and below the joint during PROM. Never force.

stem: To use a fire extinguisher correctly you follow the PASS acronym. What does the A stand for?
choice_a: Alert
choice_b: Activate
choice_c: Aim at the base of the fire
choice_d: Away from the building
rationale: PASS = Pull Aim (at base) Squeeze Sweep. Aiming at flames is ineffective.

stem: A fictional resident Mrs. Flores is on a two-person assist transfer per her care plan. Your coworker is unavailable. What should you do?
choice_a: Attempt the transfer carefully and slowly by yourself
choice_b: Skip the transfer and keep Mrs. Flores in bed until the next shift
choice_c: Wait for your coworker or find another available staff member before transferring
choice_d: Ask Mrs. Flores's family member to help you
rationale: Two-person assist means two trained staff are required. Attempting alone risks injury to both parties.

stem: The primary reason many residents fall while trying to get out of bed unassisted is:
choice_a: The bed is too low
choice_b: The resident did not ask for help
choice_c: Call lights were not answered promptly
choice_d: The resident was wearing non-skid footwear
rationale: Many falls occur when residents try to get up because call lights were not answered in a timely manner.

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: A fictional resident has a diet order for nectar-thick liquids. The CNA should:
choice_a: Give regular thin liquids if the resident requests them
choice_b: Ensure all liquids provided are thickened to the nectar consistency as ordered
choice_c: Only thicken liquids at mealtimes
choice_d: Skip the thickening if the resident is alert and oriented
rationale: All liquids must be thickened to the ordered consistency at all times to prevent aspiration.

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: Which of the following is a sign of dehydration in an older adult?
choice_a: Increased urine output
choice_b: Moist mucous membranes
choice_c: Dark urine and dry mouth
choice_d: Low blood pressure with clear urine
rationale: Dark urine and dry mouth are classic signs of dehydration in older adults.

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: Which pressure injury stage involves full-thickness skin loss with visible bone or tendon?
choice_a: Stage 1
choice_b: Stage 2
choice_c: Stage 3
choice_d: Stage 4
rationale: Stage 4 involves full-thickness tissue loss with exposed bone tendon or muscle.
source_reference: General CNA curriculum - NEEDS SME REVIEW (no confirmed standalone NATP skin integrity module uploaded)

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: Normal oxygen saturation (SpO2) for an adult is:
choice_d: 100% at all times
rationale: Normal SpO2 is 95-100%. Below 90% is a medical emergency.

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: When counting respirations the CNA should:
choice_a: Tell the resident to breathe normally then count for 30 seconds and multiply by 2
choice_b: Count chest rises for a full 60 seconds without informing the resident
choice_c: Use a stethoscope to listen to each breath
choice_d: Ask the resident to count their own breaths
rationale: Respirations are counted for a full 60 seconds without the resident's awareness as awareness changes the pattern.

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: A fictional resident Mr. Holt begins coughing repeatedly and his voice sounds wet after swallowing. This may indicate:
choice_a: Normal swallowing sounds
choice_b: Aspiration or aspiration risk
choice_c: Heartburn
choice_d: The food is too cold
rationale: Wet or gurgling voice and repeated coughing during meals are warning signs of aspiration risk.

module_title: Documentation Change of Condition and Scope
stem: Which of the following is an objective observation?
choice_a: The resident looks like she is in pain.
choice_b: I think the resident may have an infection.
choice_c: Skin on right heel is red and warm and tender to touch. Measures approximately 3 cm in diameter.
choice_d: The resident seems agitated today.
rationale: Objective observations are measurable and sensory. A specific measurement and physical description are objective.

module_title: Documentation Change of Condition and Scope
stem: If you make an error on a paper chart you should:
choice_a: Use white-out and write the correct information over it
choice_b: Scribble over the error so it is unreadable
choice_c: Draw a single line through the error then write error then initial and date
choice_d: Tear out the page and start a new one
rationale: A single line plus error plus initials plus date is the legally correct paper chart correction method.

module_title: Documentation Change of Condition and Scope
stem: A fictional resident Mrs. Rivera was alert this morning. At 2 PM she is confused and cannot state her name. This is a:
choice_a: Normal afternoon behavior in older adults
choice_b: Sign of sundowning - no reporting needed until evening
choice_c: Change of condition requiring immediate reporting to the licensed nurse
choice_d: Sign that the resident needs more activity
rationale: New confusion in a previously alert resident is a change of condition requiring immediate nursing notification.

module_title: Documentation Change of Condition and Scope
stem: Which of the following tasks is WITHIN the CNA's scope of practice?
choice_a: Administering a prescribed medication
choice_b: Inserting a urinary catheter
choice_c: Measuring and recording a resident's blood pressure
choice_d: Interpreting a physician's order
rationale: Measuring and recording vital signs including blood pressure is within CNA scope.
source_reference: NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents) Objective 1

module_title: Documentation Change of Condition and Scope
stem: The SBAR framework for reporting a change of condition stands for:
choice_a: Signs then Background then Abnormality then Response
choice_b: Situation then Background then Assessment then Recommendation
choice_c: Symptoms then Baseline then Action then Result
choice_d: Status then Background then Alert then Report
rationale: SBAR = Situation Background Assessment Recommendation.

module_title: Documentation Change of Condition and Scope
stem: A family member asks you to give their father an over-the-counter antacid. You should:
choice_a: Give it - antacids are not real medications
choice_b: Explain that all medication administration is outside CNA scope and contact the nurse
choice_c: Give half the normal dose as a compromise
choice_d: Check the internet for interactions and then decide
rationale: All medication administration including OTC is outside CNA scope.
source_reference: NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents) Objective 1

stem: A CNA repeatedly ignores a resident's call light leaving the resident soiled for over two hours on multiple occasions. This is most accurately described as:
choice_a: A single act of negligence
choice_b: Neglect
choice_c: Verbal abuse
choice_d: Involuntary seclusion
rationale: A pattern of failing to provide basic care constitutes neglect.

stem: When communicating with a resident who has aphasia after a stroke the CNA should:
choice_a: Finish the resident's sentences quickly to save time
choice_b: Speak loudly - aphasia is usually caused by hearing loss
choice_c: Be patient and allow extra time and use yes/no questions or picture boards
choice_d: Ask family members to communicate on behalf of the resident at all times
rationale: Aphasia affects language not hearing. Allow time and use simple questions and alternative communication tools.
source_reference: NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents) Objective 4

stem: A resident who is actively dying may still be able to hear you. Based on this you should:
choice_a: Avoid speaking near the resident to prevent distress
choice_b: Speak softly and calmly - hearing may be the last sense to go
choice_c: Only speak to the resident if they respond
choice_d: Speak loudly and clearly to ensure understanding
rationale: Hearing is believed to be the last sense to go. Speak softly and calmly to provide comfort.

stem: The correct principle of body mechanics when lifting states that you should:
choice_a: Bend at the waist and use your back muscles
choice_b: Keep your feet together for stability
choice_c: Bend at the knees and hips and use your leg muscles
choice_d: Twist your torso to face the direction you are moving
rationale: Bending at the knees and using leg muscles not back muscles is the core of proper body mechanics.

stem: After a resident falls what is the FIRST action the CNA should take?
choice_a: Immediately help the resident up off the floor
choice_b: Complete the incident report
choice_c: Stay calm and do not move the resident immediately and assess for injury while calling for help
choice_d: Place the resident in bed and document the fall at end of shift
rationale: Stay calm. Do not immediately lift the resident. Assess for injury and call for help first.
source_reference: NATP Module 14 (Rehabilitative/Restorative Care); NATP Module 12 (Emergency Procedures)

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: The most reliable oral temperature requires waiting how long after hot or cold beverage consumption?
choice_a: 5 minutes
choice_b: 15-30 minutes
choice_c: 1 hour
choice_d: It is not necessary to wait
rationale: A 15-30 minute wait after hot or cold intake ensures oral temperature accuracy.

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: When measuring a resident's pulse at the radial artery the CNA should use:
choice_a: The thumb which is most sensitive
choice_b: The index and middle fingers
choice_c: The entire palm for maximum contact
choice_d: A blood pressure cuff
rationale: The thumb has its own pulse and should not be used. Index and middle fingers are correct.

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: To help prevent pressure injuries the CNA should reposition an immobile resident at minimum every:
choice_a: 30 minutes
choice_b: 1 hour
choice_c: 2 hours
choice_d: 4 hours
rationale: Standard practice is repositioning at least every 2 hours for immobile residents.
source_reference: General CNA curriculum - NEEDS SME REVIEW (skin integrity - no confirmed standalone NATP upload)

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: A fictional resident's blood pressure is 88/52 at 8 AM compared to her usual 122/76. She states she feels dizzy. You should:
choice_a: Document it and check again at the next scheduled time
choice_b: Report the finding to the licensed nurse immediately
choice_c: Encourage her to drink more coffee to raise the blood pressure
choice_d: Retake the blood pressure on the other arm and then decide
rationale: Significant drop from baseline BP with symptoms is a change of condition requiring immediate nursing notification.

module_title: Documentation Change of Condition and Scope
stem: Delegation in a long-term care facility flows:
choice_a: From the CNA to the licensed nurse when the CNA is overwhelmed
choice_b: From the licensed nurse to the CNA
choice_c: From the facility administrator to any staff member
choice_d: From one CNA to another CNA of equal rank
rationale: Delegation flows from the licensed nurse to the CNA. CNAs do not delegate to other CNAs.
source_reference: NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents) Objective 3

stem: A resident with active tuberculosis should be placed on which type of precautions?
choice_a: Contact precautions only
choice_b: Droplet precautions
choice_c: Airborne precautions including a negative pressure room and N95 respirator
choice_d: Standard precautions alone are sufficient
rationale: Active TB requires airborne precautions including negative-pressure room and N95 respirator.
source_reference: General CNA infection control curriculum - NEEDS SME REVIEW (Module 1)

stem: A coworker tells you they saw another staff member slap a resident. Your coworker is afraid to report it. What should you do?
choice_a: Tell your coworker to stay out of it - it is not your business
choice_b: Report the information to the licensed nurse immediately as you also have a mandated reporting obligation
choice_c: Ask the resident if they want you to report it before doing anything
choice_d: Wait to see if the behavior happens again
rationale: As a mandated reporter you must report known or suspected abuse that comes to your attention even secondhand.

stem: A fictional resident Mr. Obi asks you to ensure his prayer items are left undisturbed on his nightstand. You should:
choice_a: Move the items to a safe location where they will not be damaged
choice_b: Explain that you cannot guarantee the nightstand items during cleaning
choice_c: Respect his request and ensure staff are aware not to disturb the items
choice_d: Tell him religious items are not allowed on the nightstand per policy
rationale: Respecting spiritual and cultural practices is part of holistic person-centered care.
source_reference: NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents) Objective 2; NATP Module 16 (Death and Dying) Objective 1

stem: In restorative care the CNA's primary approach when assisting a resident with dressing is to:
choice_a: Dress the resident completely to save time
choice_b: Encourage the resident to dress themselves as much as safely possible providing cueing and setup
choice_c: Only assist with dressing if the resident specifically requests help
choice_d: Ask the physical therapist to handle all dressing activities
rationale: Restorative care supports independence by encouraging the resident to do as much as they can safely.

module_title: Nutrition Skin Integrity and Vital Signs
stem: When feeding a resident who requires assistance the CNA should:
choice_a: Stand over the resident and feed quickly to complete the task
choice_b: Sit at the resident's eye level and offer small bites and allow adequate time between bites
choice_c: Mix all food together to save time
choice_d: Position the resident at a 30-degree recline for comfort during the meal
rationale: Proper feeding technique requires sitting at eye level offering small bites and allowing time to reduce aspiration risk.

module_title: Documentation Change of Condition and Scope
stem: Which of the following changes requires the MOST urgent report to the licensed nurse?
choice_a: A resident who ate 80% of breakfast
choice_b: A resident whose SpO2 dropped from 97% to 86%
choice_c: A resident who slept well through the night
choice_d: A resident who watched television for two hours
rationale: An SpO2 of 86% is critically low and represents a medical emergency requiring immediate nursing response.
source_reference: NATP Module 10 (Vital Signs); NATP Module 15 (Observation and Charting) Objective 5

stem: When using the RACE fire safety protocol the first step is to:
choice_a: Pull the fire alarm
choice_b: Use the fire extinguisher
choice_c: Rescue residents in immediate danger from the area
choice_d: Close all doors and windows
rationale: RACE begins with Rescue - removing residents in immediate danger.

stem: Professional boundaries require the CNA to:
choice_a: Accept small gifts from residents as a sign of appreciation
choice_b: Share personal financial problems with residents to build rapport
choice_c: Avoid accepting gifts and sharing personal problems and developing romantic relationships with residents
choice_d: Decline all conversation with residents that is not directly care-related
rationale: Professional boundaries prohibit accepting gifts and sharing personal problems and romantic relationships.

stem: Increased agitation and confusion or wandering in a resident with dementia that occurs specifically in late afternoon or early evening is called:
choice_a: Delirium
choice_b: Catastrophic reaction
choice_c: Sundowning (Sundowner Syndrome)
choice_d: Elopement behavior
rationale: Sundowning describes increased agitation in late afternoon or evening common in mid-to-late stage dementia.
source_reference: NATP Module 13 (Long Term Care Residents) Objective 5

module_title: Documentation Change of Condition and Scope
stem: The legal principle 'if you didn't document it it didn't happen' means:
choice_a: Undocumented care is assumed to have been performed unless a coworker disputes it
choice_b: Documentation is optional for routine tasks
choice_c: Care not documented in the medical record may be considered legally as though it was not provided
choice_d: You only need to document unusual events not routine care
rationale: The legal principle reflects that the medical record is the official evidence of care. Undocumented care cannot be proven.
